INFS 3822 Artificial Intelligence for Business Analytics

Week 3 Seminar

Structured Data Analysis using AI Dr. Jason Xianghua Wu

School of Information Systems & Technology Management UNSW Business School  Week 3 Seminar Recap

Structured data in the business context

Machine Learning for Structured Data Analysis

Pre-processing Structured Data for ML

Deep Learning for Structured Data Analysis

AI-Driven Structured Data Analysis to solve Business Problems

Finance Credit, Lending, and Mortgage Sector  Structured data in the business context

Structured data • Structured data refers to data that is organized in a specific format, such as a table or spreadsheet Structured data Unstructured data

Text, Photos & Videos

Emojis Location Sensor Likes data Data Shares (GPS)

Structured data collection and analysis • Easier to analyse and extract insights from structured data compared to unstructured data (e.g. text, images, audio). • Data warehousing and ETL (extract, transform, load) processes are often used to collect, store, and prepare structured data for analysis.

ML

Extract Transform Load  Examples of structured data in a business context

• Customer data, including purchase history, demographics (age, gender, income, education level, and location), and segmentation information. • Sales data, including revenue, units sold, discounts, and return information. • Inventory data, including stock levels, and supplier information. • Financial data, including income statements, balance sheets, and cash flow statements. • Marketing data, including website analytics, social media metrics, and email campaign performance.  Examples of structured data in a business context

• Human resources data, including employee information, payroll data, and performance metrics. • Supply chain data, including logistics information, shipping data, and production data. • Operational data, including machine data, sensor data, and performance metrics from production lines. • Logistics data, tracking information of orders and deliveries.

Machine Learning for Structured Data Analysis

Source: https://xkcd.com/1838/  AI-driven Business Analytics using structured data

• Machine learning algorithms can be applied to structured data to make predictions and identify patterns. • By utilizing structured data, businesses can make data-driven decisions and improve their operations and performance.

Regression Classification Ranking

Clustering Anomaly Detection

Linear Regression • Objective: Find the line of best fit through a set of data points in order to predict a numeric value • Advantages: Simplicity and ease of interpretation • Disadvantages: Assumption of linearity and a lack of ability to model more complex relationships in the data • Predict sales based on advertising spend • Predict the relationship between product prices and demand.

Example

Logistic Regression • Objective: Used for classification problems by modelling the probability of a binary outcome given a set of input features • Advantages: Simplicity, ease of interpretation, and the ability to handle nonlinearly separable data through the use of regularization • Disadvantages: Assumption of independence among features and its limited ability to model complex relationships in the data • Customer churn prediction • Determining the likelihood of a customer making a purchase based on their browsing behavior • Predict the probability of a customer responding to a marketing campaign.  Logistic Function

Decision Trees • Objective: Create a model that can predict the value of a target variable by learning simple decision rules inferred from the data features • Advantages: Interpretability, ability to handle both continuous and categorical data, and the ability to handle multi-output problems • Disadvantages: Tendency to overfit and their instability, meaning that small changes in the data can result in a very different decision tree • Customer segmentation • Determining which product to recommend to a customer based on their browsing history • Identifying which customers are most likely to default on a loan  Random Forest • Objective: Create an ensemble of decision trees to improve the predictive performance of individual decision trees by decreasing overfitting and increasing generalisation • Advantages: Ability to handle large amounts of data and a wide range of features, and ability to handle both continuous and categorical variables. • Disadvantages: Complexity and interpretability. Require more computational resources to train and evaluate • Fraud detection • Predict which customers are most likely to respond to a marketing campaign • Identify which products are most likely to sell well based on historical sales data  Pre-processing Structured Data for ML Transform the data to become suitable for ML models

• Data Cleaning • Handling Missing Values • Removing Outliers • Data Transformation • Normalization/Min-Max Scaling • Standardization/Z-score normalization • Log Transformation

• Feature Encoding • One-hot encoding Converting each categorical value into a new categorical column and assigns a 1 or 0 (True/False) value to the column.

Item Colour Item Colour_Red Colour_Blue Colour_Green Item 1 Red Item 1 1 0 0 Item 2 Blue Item 2 0 1 0 Item 3 Green Item 3 0 0 1 Item 4 Red Item 4 1 0 0

Item Colour Item 1 1 Red colour should not be Item 2 2 considered “inferior” to Item 3 3 Green colour Item 4 1

• Feature Encoding • Label Encoding Converting each categorical value in a column to a number. Useful for encoding categorical data with ordinal relationships.

Participant Height Participant Height Participant 1 Tall Participant 1 3 Participant 2 Short Participant 2 1 Participant 3 Medium Participant 3 2 Participant 4 Tall Participant 4 3

• Feature Engineering • Feature Creation Creation of new input variables based on existing data.

Customer Purchase Date Customer Weekend Purchase Days since last purchase Customer 1 2024-01-01 Customer 1 0 43 Customer 2 2024-01-13 Customer 2 1 35 Customer 3 2024-02-02 Customer 3 0 130 Customer 4 2024-02-18 Customer 4 1 55

• Feature Engineering • Feature Selection Selecting the most important features to train the model on. Techniques include: • Filter Methods: selection based on statistical measures (e.g. correlation coefficients, Chisquare test, mutual information, Fisher’s score) • Wrapper Methods: selection of a set of features as a search problem, where different combinations are prepared, evaluated, and compared with each other. (e.g. forward selection, backward elimination, and recursive feature elimination) • Embedded Methods: selection as part of the model training. Typically built into the ML algorithms such as decision trees or ensemble methods like Random Forests and Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM)

• Feature Engineering • Dimensionality Reduction Reduce the number of features in data to simplify models and reduce computation time • Principal Component Analysis (PCA) • Autoencoders

D1 D2 D3 D4

Exercise

Structured data classification using logistic regression in or

Exercise Structured data classification using logistic regression

• Objective: Train a model in Python or R to classify structured data into a Boolean outcome • Data: any CSV file that has one field/variable as a Boolean outcome variable to predict. • We will use a synthetic dataset that has 24 columns (variables), one of which is a Boolean to be used as the outcome variable (to predict). • Model type: Logistic Regression • Environment: & your choice of:

or

Exercise Introduction to Python / R

• What is Python/R • Why Python/R for ML: capabilities • How to use it? Live demo of Google Colab

Exercise Introduction to Python / R Python and R are both popular among data scientists for a number of reasons: • Versatility: Both Python and R are versatile programming languages that can be used for a wide variety of tasks, including data cleaning, exploration, visualization, and modeling. • Large and active communities: Both languages have large and active communities of users and developers, which means that there are many resources and tools available for learning and working with the languages. • Rich ecosystem of libraries and frameworks: Both languages have a rich ecosystem of libraries and frameworks for data science and machine learning, including NumPy and Pandas for data manipulation, Matplotlib and ggplot for data visualization, scikit-learn and TensorFlow for machine learning, and many more. • Good for prototyping: Both languages are easy to learn and use, and have an intuitive and interactive development environment, which makes them well-suited for prototyping and exploring data quickly.

Exercise Introduction to Python / R Python and R are both popular among data scientists for a number of reasons: • Power and performance: Python is a general-purpose language, it's easy to use and versatile, and it offers high-performance libraries such as NumPy and Pandas to process large amount of data. R is developed mainly for statistical analysis with a powerful data visualization capabilities, that's why it's commonly used among statisticians and data analysts. • Industry and academic: Both languages are widely used both in academia and industry, Python is well-known as a versatile and powerful general-purpose programming language, which makes it also a choice for software development, web development and more. R is more specialized in statistical analysis and data visualization, which makes it a must have for data scientists and statisticians. • Popularity: Python and R are both very popular in data science and machine learning, Python has been growing faster in popularity in recent years, but both are well-established languages with many resources and tools available.

Exercise How to use Python / R in Google Colab? • Google Colab, a product from Google Research. • Colab allows you to write and execute R or Python code through the browser, and is especially well suited to machine learning, data analysis and education. • Requires no setup to use, while providing access free of charge to computing resources including GPUs. • To use Google Colab, you need to be signed-in to a Google account (you can create one for free) • Visit https://colab.research.google.com/

Exercise How to use Python / R in Google Colab?

Exercise Structured data classification using logistic regression

• Objective: Train a model in Python or R to classify structured data into a Boolean outcome • Data: any CSV file that has one field/variable as a Boolean outcome variable to predict. • We will use a synthetic dataset that has 24 columns (variables), one of which is a Boolean to be used as the outcome variable (to predict). • Model type: Logistic Regression • Environment: & your choice of:

or

Exercise How to use Python / R in Google Colab? • In Google Colab, you can create Python or R notebooks to run analytics. • Notebooks can be created from scratch, or uploaded as .ipynb files. • Two notebook files are available on Moodle: • Week3_Hands_On_Exercise_Python.ipynb • Week3_Hands_On_Exercise_R.ipynb • They contain Python and R code respectively for this Hands-On exercise.

Exercise Structured data classification using logistic regression

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split data <- read.csv("data_exercise1.csv") from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y) # Load the data y <- data[,ncol(data)] data = pd.read_csv(”data_exercise1.csv") y = data["outcome"] # Split the data into training and testing sets scaler = preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X) splitIndex <- sample(1:n, size = round(0.8 * n), replace # Split the data into training and testing sets y_train <- y[splitIndex] X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, X_test <- X[-splitIndex,] test_size=0.2) y_test <- y[-splitIndex]

import pandas as pd # Load the data from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split data <- read.csv("data.csv") from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y) # Load the data y <- data[,ncol(data)] # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y) X <- scale(X) y = data["outcome"] # Split the data into training and testing sets # Preprocess the data (scaling) n <- nrow(X) scaler = preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X) splitIndex <- sample(1:n, size = round(0.8 * n), replace X = scaler.transform(X) = FALSE) First, we need to load the necessary libraries. This includes: # Split the data into training and testing sets y_train <- y[splitIndex] Python: X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y,X_test <- X[-splitIndex,] • The pandas library, which is used for data manipulation and analysis. test_size=0.2) y_test <- y[-splitIndex]

• The train_test_split function to split the data into training and testing sets. • The LogisticRegression class, which is a linear model for binary classification • The accuracy_score function which is used to measure the performance of a classifier model. R: For logistic regression in R, we do not need any libraries other than those loaded by default.

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split data <- read.csv("data.csv") from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y) # Load the data y <- data[,ncol(data)] y = data["outcome"] # Split the data into training and testing sets scaler = preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X) splitIndex <- sample(1:n, size = round(0.8 * n), replace # Split the data into training and testing sets y_train <- y[splitIndex] X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, X_test <- X[-splitIndex,] test_size=0.2) y_test <- y[-splitIndex] Next, we load the dataset from a CSV file Python: data = pd.read_csv("data.csv") reads the data from 'data.csv' file and loads into data variable using the read_csv function from the pandas library (aliased as pd). R: data <- read.csv("data.csv") reads the data from 'data.csv' file and loads into data variable using the read.csv function.

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split data <- read.csv("data.csv") from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y) # Load the data y <- data[,ncol(data)] X = data.drop(”Class", axis=1) y = data["Class"] # Split the data into training and testing sets scaler = preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X) splitIndex <- sample(1:n, size = round(0.8 * n), replace # Split the data into training and testing sets y_train <- y[splitIndex] X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, X_test <- X[-splitIndex,] test_size=0.2) Split the data into inputs and outputs. The inputs consists of all available variables (columns) in the data y_test <- y[-splitIndex]

except “Class”. The output is the variable “Class” itself. Python: data.drop(”Class", axis=1) and y = data[”Class"] takes the input and output columns from the loaded dataframe. Here the “Class” column is taken as output and rest are taken as input. R: X <- data[,-1] and y <- data[,ncol(data)] takes the input and output columns from the loaded dataframe. Here the last column is taken as output and rest are taken as input. 39

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split data <- read.csv("data.csv") from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score # Split the data into inputs (X) and outputs (y) # Load the data y <- data[,ncol(data)] y = data["outcome"] # Split the data into training and testing sets scaler = preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X) splitIndex <- sample(1:n, size = round(0.8 * n), replace # Split the data into training and testing sets y_train <- y[splitIndex] X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, X_test <- X[-splitIndex,] test_size=0.2) y_test <- y[-splitIndex]

Scale the input data to ensure that all the input features are on the same scale. It standardizes the data by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation.

# Preprocess the data (scaling) # Scale the input data scaler = preprocessing.StandardScaler().fit(X) X <- scale(X) X = scaler.transform(X) # Split the data into training and testing sets # Split the data into training and testing sets set.seed(123) # For reproducibility X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, n <- nrow(X) test_size=0.2) splitIndex <- sample(1:n, size = round(0.8 * n), replace = FALSE) y_train <- y[splitIndex] X_test <- X[-splitIndex,] y_test <- y[-splitIndex]

Split the data into training and testing sets. The training set will be used to train the model, while the testing set will be used to test the accuracy of the model. Python: Split the input and output data using the train_test_split function into training and testing set with test_size =0.2 meaning that the testing set corresponds to 20% of the data. R: splitIndex <- sample(1:n, size = round(0.8 * n), replace = FALSE) allows to split the input and output data into training and testing set with size = 0.8 * n (the training dataset is 80% of the size of the dataset, no being the number of rows). X_train <- X[splitIndex,], y_train <- y[splitIndex], X_test <- X[-splitIndex,] and y_test <- y[-splitIndex] split the input and output data into training and testing set 41

# Train a logistic regression model on the training data # Train a logistic regression model on the training data model = LogisticRegression() model <- glm(y_train ~., data = X_train, family = model.fit(X_train, y_train) binomial())

# Make predictions on the testing data # Make predictions on the testing data y_pred = model.predict(X_test) y_pred <- predict(model, newdata = X_test, type = "response") # Calculate the accuracy of the model y_pred <- ifelse(y_pred > 0.5, 1, 0) accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred) print("Accuracy:", accuracy) # Calculate the accuracy of the model accuracy <- mean(y_pred == y_test) print(paste0("Accuracy: ", accuracy))

Create a logistic regression model and train it using the training dataset Python: model = LogisticRegression() creates an instance of the LogisticRegression model. model.fit(X_train, y_train) trains the logistic regression model using the training data. R: model <- glm(y_train ~., data = X_train, family = binomial()) trains the logistic regression model using the glm function, with the formula y_train ~., data=X_train, family = binomial(). The formula is used to specify the outcome column, independent variables and the type of model. 42

# Train a logistic regression model on the training data # Train a logistic regression model on the training data model = LogisticRegression() model <- glm(y_train ~., data = X_train, family = model.fit(X_train, y_train) binomial())

# Make predictions on the testing data # Make predictions on the testing data y_pred = model.predict(X_test) y_pred <- predict(model, newdata = X_test, type = "response") # Calculate the accuracy of the model y_pred <- ifelse(y_pred > 0.5, 1, 0) accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred) print("Accuracy:", accuracy) # Calculate the accuracy of the model accuracy <- mean(y_pred == y_test) print(paste0("Accuracy: ", accuracy))

Makes predictions on the testing data. Python: model.predict function is used to predict the outcome of the testing data (X_test). R: y_pred <- predict(model, newdata = X_test, type = "response") makes predictions on the testing data using predict function, with newdata = X_test and type = "response" to get the probability of outcome. y_pred <- ifelse(y_pred > 0.5, 1, 0) threshold the probability values to get binary values, if probability is greater than 0.5 then it's 1 otherwise 0

# Train a logistic regression model on the training data # Train a logistic regression model on the training data model = LogisticRegression() model <- glm(y_train ~., data = X_train, family = model.fit(X_train, y_train) binomial())

# Make predictions on the testing data # Make predictions on the testing data y_pred = model.predict(X_test) y_pred <- predict(model, newdata = X_test, type = "response") # Calculate the accuracy of the model y_pred <- ifelse(y_pred > 0.5, 1, 0) accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred) print("Accuracy:", accuracy) # Calculate the accuracy of the model accuracy <- mean(y_pred == y_test) print(paste0("Accuracy: ", accuracy))

Calculate the accuracy of the model by comparing the predictions with the true values in y_test Python: accuracy = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred) calculates the accuracy of the model. print allows to display the calculated accuracy. R: accuracy <- mean(y_pred == y_test) calculates the accuracy of the model by comparing the predictions with the true values in y_test. print(paste0("Accuracy: ", accuracy)) prints the accuracy of the model.  Deep Learning for Structured Data Analysis

Deep Neural Networks

Deep vs. Traditional Machine Learning. • Traditional ML: Statistical analysis to predict outcomes • e.g. linear regression, logistic regression, decision trees, etc. • Requires extensive feature engineering • Typically handles less complex problems • Offers greater interpretability • Requires less computational power • Deep Learning: artificial neural networks designed to recognize patterns • Inspired by the structure and function of the brain's neural networks • Minimizes the need for feature engineering • Capable of handling more complex problems • ”Black box" models makes them less transparent and harder to explain • Requires significant computational resources • Inspired by the brain's neural networks, organised in layers of neurons

Source: http://neuralnetworksanddeeplearning.com/chap5.html

• Weights and biases in a neural network • Goal: convert an input into an output • Weights: numerical parameters determine how strongly each neuron affects the others. • Biases: additional parameter that is added to the weighted sum (similar to an intercept)

Source: https://towardsdatascience.com/whats-the-role-of-weights-and-bias-in-a-neural-network-4cf7e9888a0f 48 • Fully connected Neural Networks • Basic form of neural network • Each neuron in one layer is connected to all neurons in the next layer • Versatile and can be used for a wide range of tasks • Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) / Long Short-Term Memory Networks (LSTMs) • Well-suited for time-series data • Capture patterns across time steps • Ideal for forecasting and sequence prediction tasks • Transformers • Originally designed for natural language processing • Attention mechanisms to weigh the importance of different parts of the data • Highly effective for complex relationships within the data

• Transfer learning and fine-tuning • Leverage/Reuse a pretrained model as starting point for a model on a different task • Select a Pretrained Model • Modify the Model for your specific task (e.g. change the final layer(s) of the model to match the number of outputs required for the new task) • Freeze the Weights of earlier layers • Train the Model on the new data

• Ensemble methods • Approach: combine multiple models to improve the overall performance, accuracy, and robustness of predictions • Principle: a group of weak models can be combined to form a strong model • Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) – e.g. Random Forest • Combine multiple models of the same type trained on different subsets of the same training data, selected with replacement. • The final output is obtained by averaging the predictions (for regression tasks) or by majority voting (for classification tasks) • Boosting – e.g. Gradient Boosting, XGBoost • Train models sequentially where each model attempts to correct the errors made by the previous models. • The final prediction is a weighted sum of all the models' predictions (model weighted are based on their accuracy).

Artificial Intelligence in Finance AI application areas in finance

Fig. 7.1 AI application areas in finance, p.108 Source: Textbook - Applied Artificial Intelligence in Business: Concepts and Cases https://primoa.library.unsw.edu.au/permalink/f/jhud33/UNSW_ALMA51331715730001731

Artificial Intelligence in Finance

Investment Banking • Goal: assists companies and governments in raising capital • Challenge: Investment banks need to extract insights from large and complex datasets to make informed business decisions. • AI can help investment bankers to: • Analyse market data and identify profitable investment opportunities • Automate repetitive tasks such as data entry and financial modelling • Identify patterns and trends in financial data that may indicate potential risks or opportunities • Optimize trading strategies and make more accurate predictions about market movements • Automate compliance and regulatory tasks, helping banks to reduce the risk of noncompliance

Artificial Intelligence in Finance

Personalised Finance • Generative AI for Customer Interactions in Banking
